frameworks.name: drift-diffusion / evidence accumulation
frameworks.one_line: Decision variable accumulates noisy evidence to a bound for choice.

frameworks.name: adaptive history bias
frameworks.one_line: Trial-history bias adjusts to environmental correlation structure.

frameworks.name: starting-point vs accumulation-bias dichotomy
frameworks.one_line: Distinguishes baseline offset from biased build-up rate of DV.

core_question: Do adaptive history biases shape the build-up rate (not baseline) of action-selective motor cortical activity during decision formation?
key_assumptions: Motor beta lateralization functions as a neural marker of the decision variable
History biases can be captured as linear combination of recent stimuli and choices
Build-up rate vs starting point of DV are dissociable neural signatures
manipulations: Repetition probability of stimulus category across blocks (repetitive 0.8 / neutral 0.5 / alternating 0.2)
Trial-by-trial history bias inferred from behavioral model
key_findings: Participants flexibly adapted history biases to environmental correlation structure
Adaptive history bias selectively shaped build-up of action-selective motor cortical activity, not baseline
This bias effect added to the impact of current sensory evidence
Bias adjustment uniquely predicted individual task performance beyond sensitivity
interpretations: Motor cortical action-plan build-up is shaped by dynamic prior expectations from environmental adaptation
Adaptive priors enter decision formation via accumulation bias rather than starting-point offset
